response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a delivery driver in Philadelphia, I've seen how the pandemic has changed everything—my route, my job, even how people talk about health. With my depression and the way I spend hours on social media, I've been hit hard by the constant arguments online about vaccines. My parents were educated, but I never went to college, so I don't have the same tools to navigate these conversations. When I see posts saying vaccines are dangerous or that they're just scams, I feel stuck. I used to play board games with my wife, but now I'm more focused on my screen than our little moments. I try to stay calm, but the low agreeableness and high conscientiousness make it hard to disagree without getting defensive. My religious beliefs as a Christian tell me to follow science, but the noise online feels overwhelming. I think the best way to manage this is to limit social media time, talk to my wife about my feelings, and focus on what matters most: staying healthy and being present. If I can find peace in my routine, maybe I can handle these discussions better without letting them take over my life.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 38-year-old married delivery driver with no formal education but educated parents. They're depressed, spend hours on social media, and have low agreeableness and high conscientiousness. They're Christian, which influences their view on science and health. The opinion focuses on practical steps: limiting social media, talking to their spouse, and prioritizing mental health. The tone is personal, reflective, and grounded in their daily life in Philadelphia.
response_parsed.tone: Reflective, grounded in personal experience, slightly weary but hopeful
